---
title: ""
description: ""
listing: false
---

::: {.hero}
::: {.hero-text}
# Rahul Manavalan

I work on variational formulations, their associated optimization flows, and structure-preserving numerical algorithms for problems in scientific computing and inverse problems. 

Broadly, my interests span:

- Scientific Computing 
- Mathematical Physics
- Numerical Analysis
- Uncertainty quantification
- Inverse Problems

For more details, visit:


[Research](research.qmd){.btn .btn-primary} [Publications](publications.qmd){.btn .btn-primary} [Blog](blog.qmd){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

![](assets/img/images.jpeg){width=100%}

:::

## Selected research directions

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Flow-based solvers

How can one derive and classify iterative solvers for solving **linear algebraic systems** and **saddle point systems** as discretizations of continuous dynamical systems?
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Kernel methods

How can one construct **RBF kernels** that **exactly** satisfy **Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions**? 
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Inverse Problems and PDEs

How can **PDEs that arise from inverse problems** be used to obtain **theoretical guarantees** on the numerical methods that solve the corresponding inverse problems?
:::
:::

## Recent news

```{=html}
<div class="callout-note">
  <strong>(July 2026) New preprint:</strong> Our paper on <i>Krylov smoothers for solving linear systems</i> is on <i>arxiv</i>.
</div>

```

## Latest blog posts

```{=html}
<div class="post-grid">
  <a class="post-card" href="posts/gradient-flows-linear-solvers/">
    <span class="post-tag">Numerical analysis</span>
    <h3>Gradient Flows for Linear Solvers</h3>
    <p>A short note on viewing classical iterative methods as discretized flows.</p>
  </a>
  <a class="post-card" href="posts/nonlinear-rkhs-regression/">
    <span class="post-tag">Dynamical systems</span>
    <h3>Nonlinear RKHS Regression as a Flow</h3>
    <p>Sketching a dissipative PDE viewpoint for nonlinear regression.</p>
  </a>
</div>
```
